narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting their chances of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 28, full-time enrollment plans, and school decile rankings from your early years—and calculated a 76.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate without issues. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year and stood out starkly against the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile ranking, also a 1, which similarly signaled early academic struggles compared to the average applicant's stronger start. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 provided a slight counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since it's not far below the dataset average of 3.21, but it wasn't enough to offset the dominant impact of those low decile rankings. Overall, your academic deciles were the top drivers here, far outweighing other elements like your LSAT or family income, because they directly reflect sustained performance patterns linked to bar exam outcomes in the model's analysis. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles with stronger test scores, your profile aligned more closely with those predicted to struggle. This doesn't define your potential, but it explains the model's cautious stance. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the average 36.7, strengthening your early-year academics through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria. Building a narrative of recent improvement in your application essays could also highlight your resilience as a first-generation or middle-income aspirant. With targeted preparation, you can shift these odds in your favor for the next cycle.
